item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving this arc

item.title: Inteviews
item.content:encoded:
I can't say I'm very hooked. What I did find interesting was that you talked about what parts he was made of, but after that I quickly lost interest.

The interviews however, I don't like. The wording is awkward and unnatural, and seems forced by the writer.

About the transcription itself.
Transcriptions are written versions of something that has been said orally.
In interview 1, the SCP starts by clearing its throat. This in itself is fine, but you didn't transcribe the pauses from both sides. Instead of "…" you could write "X person pauses for a moment" - or similar. This can help build atmosphere when done properly.

The wording you use is awkward and unnatural, mostly from the SCP. He is rather inconsistent. For example, in one line, he answers in a controlled and specific manner. The next he's comes off as cryptic and confused. This really doesn't seem something any person would do. When people converse, they do not do complete 180-emotion flips. They usually hold a stance in the conversation, and that reflects, they're consistent.

When you refer to the SCP in the interviews, you use "it", instead of "he". I get that it's procedure, but he's not an object, he's a humanoid. His gender has already been decided earlier in the document, so to use "it" is wrong. It's either "he" or "SCP-X".

When you refer to what they do (actions) in the interview, it only seems to happen when it is relevant, like "SCP-2970 squints at the mention of artifacts" seems only to be there, because you intended it, not because he actually squinted. By the way, transcription can't be all-knowing. You don't know if it was the mention of the artifacts that made him squint. It could be something entirely different. If you have to mention it, "Upon mentioning the artifacts, SCP-X visibly squints", or "SCP-X visibly squints" would be better.
That's what I mean when I said it seems forced by you.

There's more on the interviews, but I think that's enough to chew on for the time being. The SCP document itself doesn't actually interest me, but I'm sure there are others that do find it interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In terms of a narrative that develops in the space of a mainlist article, this is very well-constructed. The story is told in a way that feels natural to the format, and walks the line between standalone piece and portion of a larger story arc successfully.

The story itself continues to be engaging as well. The imagery of the eye-capture scene is pretty reminiscent of other mythologies that are already well-established around here, but nevertheless it was written in such a way that I was interested in finding out what happens next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nevermind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Looks like you have the reasoning right. It's standard procedure to refer to all SCPs by number, even if they are sentient or human. It's the same reason a butcher wouldn't name a cow. The job doesn't include room for empathy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was initially iffy about an apparently random super robot humanoid, but the missionary aspect — and the logs — won me over. I really liked the voice of the character. (Oddly, the polar opposite of the above comment! Who knows. Wasn't on purpose, bu an odd coincidence.)

I'm interested to see where this might go in future stories; if anything, my critique is more that it feels slightly incomplete. Unsure if that's a bad thing or not; not every story has an end. (Also, I haven't finished reading all the MTF entries associated with this.)

+1

EDIT: Now that I've finished, I strongly recommend linking Samsara from the text of the SCP at the end. Maybe with a doofy "ERROR: You are not allowed to access the SAMSARA file." or whatnot. But it really completes a piece that feels incomplete without it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, anyone who knows me could tell you that I have a soft spot in my heart for cyborgs and robots, so when I began reading this article I was quite intrigued. Unfortunatley, that intrigue didn't really last, as I didn't find the abilities of the object to be particularly interesting.

Worse, I felt that the dialogue in the interviews was rather stilted. Granted, I could let that slide for the cyborg, after all, it would make a little bit of sense for his words to seem stiff and unnatural, but the researcher's dialogue also was stilted, and that doesn't gel.

Additionally, I have to wonder why they call the SCP its object number rather than just ask it its name and call it that. Yes, the SCP is cold and objects are to be treated as objects, but calling an intelligent being by its name generally garners more cooperation from said being than slapping some number on it.

-1.

EDIT: Got a chance to look back over the updated dialogue. You did a pretty good job addressing most of my issues with the piece (sneaky way of getting around the whole calling the object by its SCP number, btw). As such, I've changed my vote.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My thoughts too.
The article is well made, but the Foundation should be very cautious with sentient SCPs as they could trick them.

Though i realy like that it did not actualy turned hostile and attacked/killed the staff.
He is not a bad cyborg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you very much for going through it! All of those should be approximately fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is done, thanks for the feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
although I should be thanking sirpudding for that

I don't have the slightest clue what I did. We had planned for these things to be connected from the beginning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding Titan. I think it would make the article significantly easier to read/navigate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article! Just one tiny, tiny thing. Could you put the interviews and the incident log in collapsibles? I feel like it might navigating the page easier, since it would require less scrolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Yeah, here's everything I noticed...
- hide block

fed with standard humanoid procedures.

Containment procedures aren't very filling. 'via' would be better than 'with'.

although it claims this function…have been "locked".

SCP-2970 cannot fall unconscious or asleep,

This is just kind of awkward.

evidence of anomalous phenomena were observed

Following the events of Operation AZURE PEREGRINE, it was decided that the risk of residual anomalies from the existence of SCP-2970's cult was too high, and the potential benefits of full access to SCP-2970's information outweighed the risks.

This line confuses me. If the risks are too high, then how does the potential outweigh the risks? I think a word was left out or in.

SCP-2970 slowly raises its prosthetic hand into the view.

Drop 'the'.

SCP-2970 flexes the hand and it slowly begins to exhibit a white glow. SCP-2970 obscures the village from view behind its transparent hand, and the image of the village through the hand becomes gradually distorted.

This is a little overwritten. "flexes the hand, which begins to glow white." for instance.

All bullets fired at SCP-2970 divert away shortly before impact, instead being deflected and colliding with surrounding objects.

Redundancy! "All bullets fired at SCP-2970 are deflected shortly before impact and collide with surrounding objects."

So I guess, overall, it's not as bad as I made it sound. :B There were just two or three articles in a row last night with notable proofreading errors.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean, post-operation, where it might be side-effects of the operation still active.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do you mean disarmed? Unless you mean literally removing his prosthetic arm, he didn't really have anything for them to disarm :P He was restrained anyhow, and couldn't move during the operation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always imagined the eye to be a separate storage system. Anyway, I suppose the Foundation could've tried to disarm 2790 prior to the operation to ensure things don't go south.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I mean… it's kind of hard to access memories when the eye is no longer attached to the brain from which it pulls memories. So taking out the eye wouldn't work. You can't get the memories without leaving him intact, awake, and fixing the brain, which is exactly what they did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Usually I don't do this, but I will quote from a user (kyew) from the SCP reddit (which was talking about your article, and I agree with the quoted portion).

I also don't think the Foundation would reactivate an untrusted entity's powers. At best they would remove the guy's eye and try to activate it independently.

I still cannot reconcile with that, and that in-universe misstep made me do the downvote, especially an interview hints that it may not be necessarily trustworthy to begin with.

On the other hand, at least I am glad you have rectified the discrepancy of why the Foundation want to activate the eye (although I should be thanking sirpudding for that).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Actually, he was referring to the lack of David Tennant in the distant past.

But seriously, though, thank you. Were any other specific sections you noticed that could be cleaned up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting, I like the "ancient cyborg" angle and the story suggested by his eye projections. That said, writing needs a little work. For instance: 'tenets', not 'tenants'.

item.title: Holy Misplacement.
item.content:encoded:
Part 3 of a series.

Thanks to my teammates A Random Day, GreenWolf, and sirpudding for providing feedback and support. Also thanks to TwistedGears, MrWrong, and everyone else who read it in chat, for feedback.

EDIT: Collapsed the addenda and fixed the issues TL333s pointed out. Thanks!
EDIT2: Made some changes to Dr. Acosta's dialogue based on Conwell's suggestions.

Other entries:
1. Avatara, by A Random Day
2. GRANT REQUEST FOR INVESTIGATING THE APPLICATION OF CERTAIN RESEARCH ASSETS IN OVERCOMING INHERENT LIMITATIONS OF THE HUMAN BODY by GreenWolf
3. SCP-2970, Holy Misplacement, by TyGently
4. Operation AZURE PEREGRINE, by sirpudding
5. The Lord of Endowments, by sirpudding
6. Samsara, by TyGently and A Random Day
